---
editor:
    render-on-save: true
# bibliography: references.bib
csl: ieee.csl
format:
  html:
    filters: [tikz.lua, strip-yaml-frontmatter.lua, maintext-filter.lua, sitext-filter.lua]
---

# Data-informed prior for the biological mean expression {#sec-mean-anchoring}

In @sec-capture-prior, we introduced a biology-informed prior for the capture
probability $\nu_c$ that resolved the first source of practical
non-identifiability in the variable capture probability (VCP) model: the
degeneracy between the capture efficiency and the total biological expression.
The key idea was to anchor $\nu_c$ using the known relationship
$\nu_c \approx L_c / M_0$, where $L_c$ is the observed library size and $M_0$
is the expected total mRNA content per cell.

In this section, we address a **second source of practical non-identifiability**
that persists even after the capture prior has been applied: the degeneracy
between the biological mean expression $\mu_g$ and the overdispersion parameter
$\phi_g$ (or equivalently $p_g$) within the negative binomial distribution. We
develop a data-informed prior on $\mu_g$ that resolves this degeneracy by
exploiting a concentration-of-measure argument over cells---directly analogous
to how the capture prior exploits concentration over genes.

## The residual identifiability problem {#sec-mean-residual-identifiability}

### The expected count under the VCP model

Recall from @sec-dirichlet-multinomial and @sec-denoising that, under the
mean-odds parameterization, the expected observed UMI count for gene $g$ in
cell $c$ is

$$
\langle u_{gc} \rangle = \mu_g \cdot \nu_c,
$${#eq-mean-expected-count}

where $\mu_g$ is the biological mean expression (the expected number of mRNA
molecules for gene $g$ in the cell) and $\nu_c$ is the capture probability for
cell $c$. The negative binomial parameters are related to $\mu_g$ through the
mean-odds parameterization as

$$
r_g = \mu_g \cdot \phi, \qquad p = \frac{1}{1 + \phi},
$${#eq-mean-nb-params}

where $\phi$ is the odds ratio parameter that controls the overdispersion.

### What the capture prior constrains

The biology-informed capture prior from @sec-capture-prior constrains
$\nu_c \approx L_c / M_0$, effectively pinning the capture probability for
each cell. After this constraint is applied, the expected count
@eq-mean-expected-count becomes

$$
\langle u_{gc} \rangle \approx \mu_g \cdot \frac{L_c}{M_0}.
$${#eq-mean-expected-count-anchored}

The ratio $L_c / M_0$ is a known quantity for each cell (computed from the
observed data and the biological constant $M_0$). Therefore, the expected count
is now controlled by a **single unknown per gene**: $\mu_g$.

### What remains unconstrained

While the expected count constrains $\mu_g$, the full shape of the negative
binomial distribution depends on **two** parameters: the dispersion $r_g$ and
the success probability $p$ (or equivalently, $\mu_g$ and $\phi$). The
variance of the observed count is

$$
\text{Var}(u_{gc}) = \langle u_{gc} \rangle \cdot
\frac{1}{\hat{p}_c},
$${#eq-mean-variance}

where $\hat{p}_c = p / (\nu_c + p(1 - \nu_c))$ is the effective success
probability after capture thinning (see @eq-umicounts-marginal-nb-p). The
variance-to-mean ratio is $1/\hat{p}_c$, which depends on both $p$ (or $\phi$)
and $\nu_c$.

This means that the data constrain $\mu_g$ primarily through the **first
moment** (the mean) and $\phi$ primarily through the **second moment** (the
variance-to-mean ratio). However, the second moment is inherently noisier to
estimate than the first: the sample variance has variance proportional to the
fourth central moment, while the sample mean has variance proportional to only
the second moment. This asymmetry creates a **practical non-identifiability**:
the data strongly constrain $\mu_g$ but only weakly constrain $\phi$.

### The likelihood ridge

To make this precise, consider the negative binomial log-likelihood for a
single gene $g$ observed across $C$ cells with counts
$\underline{u}_g = (u_{g1}, u_{g2}, \ldots, u_{gC})$. In the mean-odds
parameterization, the log-likelihood as a function of $(\mu_g, \phi)$ is

$$
\ell(\mu_g, \phi \mid \underline{u}_g) = \sum_{c=1}^C
\log \text{NB}(u_{gc} \mid r_g = \mu_g \phi, \; \hat{p}_c(\phi, \nu_c)).
$${#eq-mean-loglik}

The maximum likelihood surface in $(\mu_g, \phi)$ space exhibits a
characteristic **ridge**: there is a well-defined maximum along the $\mu_g$
direction (the mean is tightly constrained), but the curvature along the $\phi$
direction is much weaker (the overdispersion is loosely constrained). In
variational inference with a mean-field guide, this manifests as a broad,
poorly-resolved posterior for $\phi$ that can drift during optimization without
substantially changing the ELBO.

### Consequences for cross-dataset comparison

When two datasets from the same biological condition are fitted
**independently**, each model finds its own point on the likelihood ridge.
Because the ridge is broad in the $\phi$ direction, the two models may settle
on different $\phi$ values. In the canonical parameterization, where $r_g$ and
$p$ are jointly constrained by the mean, different $\phi$ (equivalently $p$)
values lead to different $r_g$ estimates even when the underlying biology is
identical. This is why the inferred means can show poor correspondence across
independently-fitted models despite excellent posterior predictive checks
within each model: the mean is well-calibrated within each model, but the
parameter decomposition into $(r_g, p)$ or $(\mu_g, \phi)$ differs.

### Consequences for the hierarchical $\phi_g$ prior

In the hierarchical gene-specific $\phi_g$ model from @sec-hierarchical-p
(adapted to the mean-odds parameterization), each gene draws its own $\phi_g$
from a population distribution:

$$
\log(\phi_g) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\phi, \sigma_\phi).
$${#eq-mean-hier-phi}

Since $\phi_g$ only affects the overdispersion (not the mean) in the mean-odds
parameterization, the hierarchical prior on $\phi_g$ has no leverage on the
mean structure. The optimizer can find configurations where different genes have
very different $\phi_g$ values that all produce similar likelihoods, creating
$G$ independent degeneracy manifolds. This explains why the mean-odds
parameterization can fail with hierarchical $\phi_g$ even when the canonical
parameterization (where $p_g$ affects both the mean and the variance)
succeeds: the canonical parameterization's coupling between $p_g$ and the
mean provides implicit regularization that the mean-odds parameterization
lacks.

## The concentration argument {#sec-mean-concentration}

### Sample mean as an estimator of $\mu_g$

Consider the sample mean of gene $g$ across all $C$ cells:

$$
\bar{u}_g = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^C u_{gc}.
$${#eq-mean-sample-mean}

Under the model, the expected value of this quantity is

$$
\langle \bar{u}_g \rangle = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^C \langle u_{gc} \rangle =
\frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^C \mu_g \cdot \nu_c = \mu_g \cdot \bar{\nu},
$${#eq-mean-sample-mean-expectation}

where

$$
\bar{\nu} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^C \nu_c
$${#eq-mean-nu-bar}

is the average capture probability across all cells in the dataset. Given the
capture prior, $\bar{\nu}$ is approximately a known quantity (it can be
computed from the library sizes and $M_0$). Therefore, the sample mean
$\bar{u}_g$ is an unbiased estimator of $\mu_g \cdot \bar{\nu}$, and
inverting gives

$$
\hat{\mu}_g = \frac{\bar{u}_g}{\bar{\nu}}
$${#eq-mean-mu-hat}

as the natural point estimator of $\mu_g$.

### Variance of the sample mean

To quantify how precisely $\bar{u}_g$ pins $\mu_g$, we compute its variance.
Since the cells are independent (conditional on the model parameters), the
variance of $\bar{u}_g$ is

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{u}_g) = \frac{1}{C^2} \sum_{c=1}^C \text{Var}(u_{gc}).
$${#eq-mean-var-sample-mean}

For a negative binomial random variable $u_{gc} \sim \text{NB}(r_g,
\hat{p}_c)$, the variance is

$$
\text{Var}(u_{gc}) = \frac{r_g \hat{p}_c}{(1 - \hat{p}_c)^2}
= \langle u_{gc} \rangle \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \hat{p}_c},
$${#eq-mean-nb-variance}

where we used the fact that the mean of the negative binomial (in our
convention) is $r_g \hat{p}_c / (1 - \hat{p}_c)$. The factor $1/(1 -
\hat{p}_c)$ is the variance-to-mean ratio, which quantifies the
overdispersion.

For a useful approximation, we note that $\hat{p}_c = p / (\nu_c + p(1 -
\nu_c))$, and for typical single-cell experiments where $\nu_c \ll 1$, we have
$\hat{p}_c \approx p / \nu_c$ when $p$ is not too close to zero. In this
regime, $1 - \hat{p}_c \approx 1$, and the variance simplifies to

$$
\text{Var}(u_{gc}) \approx \mu_g \nu_c + \frac{(\mu_g \nu_c)^2}{r_g}.
$${#eq-mean-nb-variance-approx}

The first term is the Poisson contribution (shot noise), and the second is the
overdispersion contribution scaled by $1/r_g$. Substituting into
@eq-mean-var-sample-mean and using the approximation that all cells have
similar capture probabilities $\nu_c \approx \bar{\nu}$, we obtain

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{u}_g) \approx \frac{1}{C}
\left(\mu_g \bar{\nu} + \frac{(\mu_g \bar{\nu})^2}{r_g}\right).
$${#eq-mean-var-sample-mean-approx}

### Coefficient of variation

The coefficient of variation (CV) of $\bar{u}_g$ relative to its expectation
$\mu_g \bar{\nu}$ is

$$
\text{CV}(\bar{u}_g) = \frac{\sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{u}_g)}}{\mu_g \bar{\nu}}
\approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{C}} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\mu_g \bar{\nu}} + \frac{1}{r_g}}.
$${#eq-mean-cv}

This is the key result. The CV decreases as $1/\sqrt{C}$ with the number of
cells, and depends on two gene-specific quantities: the expected observed
count $\mu_g \bar{\nu}$ (controlling the Poisson noise) and the dispersion
$r_g$ (controlling the overdispersion noise).

### Numerical examples

To assess the magnitude of this CV for realistic parameters, consider a
dataset with $C = 1{,}000$ cells and average capture probability
$\bar{\nu} = 0.1$ (corresponding to $M_0 = 200{,}000$ and median library
size $\approx 20{,}000$).

**Case 1: Low-expression, high-overdispersion gene.** With $\mu_g = 10$ and
$r_g = 1$ (strong overdispersion):

$$
\text{CV}(\bar{u}_g) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1{,}000}}
\sqrt{\frac{1}{10 \times 0.1} + \frac{1}{1}}
= \frac{1}{31.6} \sqrt{1 + 1}
\approx 4.5\%.
$${#eq-mean-cv-case1}

**Case 2: Moderate-expression gene.** With $\mu_g = 100$ and $r_g = 5$:

$$
\text{CV}(\bar{u}_g) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1{,}000}}
\sqrt{\frac{1}{100 \times 0.1} + \frac{1}{5}}
= \frac{1}{31.6} \sqrt{0.1 + 0.2}
\approx 1.7\%.
$${#eq-mean-cv-case2}

**Case 3: High-expression gene.** With $\mu_g = 1{,}000$ and $r_g = 10$:

$$
\text{CV}(\bar{u}_g) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1{,}000}}
\sqrt{\frac{1}{1{,}000 \times 0.1} + \frac{1}{10}}
= \frac{1}{31.6} \sqrt{0.01 + 0.1}
\approx 1.0\%.
$${#eq-mean-cv-case3}

Even for the worst case (low expression, high overdispersion), the sample mean
pins $\mu_g \bar{\nu}$ to within $\approx 5\%$. For moderately and highly
expressed genes, the precision is $1$--$2\%$. With $C = 5{,}000$ cells
(common in modern datasets), these values improve by a factor of $\sqrt{5}$,
yielding sub-percent precision for all but the most extreme genes.

### Comparison with the capture prior's concentration argument

The capture prior in @sec-capture-prior exploited the law of large numbers
**over genes** within a single cell: the total mRNA count $M_c = \sum_{g=1}^G
m_g$ concentrates around its expectation with $\text{CV}(M_c) = 1/\sqrt{r_T
p} \approx 0.5\%$ for mammalian cells with $G \approx 20{,}000$ genes.

The mean anchoring prior exploits the law of large numbers **over cells**
within a single gene: the sample mean $\bar{u}_g$ concentrates around its
expectation with $\text{CV}(\bar{u}_g) \approx 1$--$5\%$ for typical
single-cell datasets with $C \approx 1{,}000$--$10{,}000$ cells.

The two arguments are complementary. The capture prior uses concentration over
the gene dimension (large $G$) to anchor the cell-level parameter $\nu_c$.
The mean anchoring prior uses concentration over the cell dimension (large
$C$) to anchor the gene-level parameter $\mu_g$. Together, they constrain
both factors in the product $\mu_g \cdot \nu_c$ that determines the expected
count.

## The data-informed prior on $\mu_g$ {#sec-mean-prior}

### Prior specification

Given the concentration argument of the previous section, we define the
**data-informed prior** on the biological mean expression as follows. First,
compute the empirical anchor for each gene:

$$
\hat{\mu}_g = \frac{\bar{u}_g + \epsilon}{\bar{\nu}},
$${#eq-mean-anchor-def}

where $\bar{u}_g$ is the observed sample mean for gene $g$, $\bar{\nu}$ is
the average capture probability (computed from the library sizes and $M_0$ as
in @sec-capture-prior), and $\epsilon > 0$ is a small pseudocount that
prevents $\log(0)$ for unexpressed genes (typically $\epsilon \approx 10^{-3}$).

The prior on $\mu_g$ is then a log-normal distribution centered on this
anchor:

$$
\log(\mu_g) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\log(\hat{\mu}_g), \; \sigma_\mu^2\right),
$${#eq-mean-prior-def}

where $\sigma_\mu > 0$ controls the tightness of the anchor. Equivalently,
$\mu_g$ has a log-normal distribution with geometric mean $\hat{\mu}_g$ and
log-scale $\sigma_\mu$.

### Interpretation of $\sigma_\mu$

The hyperparameter $\sigma_\mu$ plays the same role for the mean anchor as
$\sigma_M$ plays for the capture prior: it controls how tightly the prior
constrains the parameter to its data-implied value.

- **Small $\sigma_\mu$ (e.g., $0.1$--$0.2$):** Strong anchor. The prior
  tightly constrains $\mu_g$ to within $\sim 10$--$20\%$ of the
  data-implied value (since $e^{0.2} \approx 1.22$). Appropriate when the
  dataset has many cells and the sample mean is very precise.

- **Moderate $\sigma_\mu$ (e.g., $0.3$--$0.5$):** The recommended default.
  Allows $\sim 1.3$--$1.6\times$ fold variation around the anchor. This
  accommodates the finite-sample noise in $\bar{u}_g$ and the approximation
  in $\bar{\nu}$, while still providing strong regularization against the
  $\mu_g$--$\phi_g$ degeneracy.

- **Large $\sigma_\mu$ (e.g., $> 1$):** Weak anchor. The prior provides
  mild regularization but does not strongly constrain $\mu_g$. In the limit
  $\sigma_\mu \to \infty$, the prior becomes flat (uninformative) and the
  anchoring effect disappears, recovering the standard model.

### Relationship to empirical Bayes

The data-informed prior is a form of **empirical Bayes**: the prior
hyperparameters (the per-gene centers $\log \hat{\mu}_g$) are estimated from
the data. This is standard practice in genomics---DESeq2 estimates dispersion
priors from marginal gene-level statistics, edgeR computes a common dispersion
from data, and limma-voom learns the mean-variance relationship empirically.

The key requirement for empirical Bayes to be valid is that the prior should be
estimated from summary statistics that are approximately sufficient for the
hyperparameters and approximately independent of the gene-level parameters
being estimated. In our case, the anchor $\hat{\mu}_g$ is derived from the
sample mean, which is a sufficient statistic for $\mu_g$ in the negative
binomial family (conditional on the overdispersion). The full likelihood
constrains the posterior through the complete count distribution (all moments),
so the prior center provides a starting point that the model refines using
overdispersion information. There is no circularity: we use the first moment
(mean) to center the prior, and the likelihood uses the full distribution
(including higher moments) to determine the posterior.

### Gene subsetting

When the model is fitted on a subset of genes (e.g., the 2,000 most variable),
the anchor $\hat{\mu}_g$ is computed from the observed means of the selected
genes, and $\bar{\nu}$ should be adjusted as described in
@sec-cap-gene-subset. When fitting the full transcriptome, no adjustment is
needed.

## Implied prior on $r_g$ {#sec-mean-implied-r}

The mean anchor constrains $\mu_g$ but not $\phi_g$ (or $p_g$). Since the
dispersion parameter $r_g$ depends on both, the anchor has different
implications for each parameterization.

### Mean-odds parameterization

In the mean-odds parameterization, $r_g = \mu_g \cdot \phi$. With $\mu_g$
anchored at $\hat{\mu}_g$, the dispersion becomes

$$
r_g \approx \hat{\mu}_g \cdot \phi,
$${#eq-mean-r-meanodds}

and is determined entirely by $\phi$ (scalar or gene-specific $\phi_g$). The
mean and overdispersion are now cleanly separated: $\mu_g$ controls the mean,
$\phi$ controls the shape. There is no residual degeneracy.

### Mean-probability parameterization

In the mean-probability parameterization, $r_g = \mu_g \cdot (1 - p) / p$.
With $\mu_g$ anchored:

$$
r_g \approx \hat{\mu}_g \cdot \frac{1 - p}{p},
$${#eq-mean-r-meanprob}

and $r_g$ is again determined by the single remaining free parameter $p$.

### Canonical parameterization

In the canonical parameterization, the gene-specific parameter is $r_g$
directly. The mean is $\mu_g = r_g \cdot (1 - p) / p$ (or equivalently
$r_g / \phi$ in the odds formulation). Anchoring the mean implies

$$
r_g \approx \hat{\mu}_g \cdot \frac{p}{1 - p} = \frac{\hat{\mu}_g}{\phi},
$${#eq-mean-r-canonical}

which constrains $r_g$ given $\phi$ (or $p$). In this parameterization, the
anchor is applied to $r_g$ directly, with $\hat{r}_g = \hat{\mu}_g / \phi_0$
for some reference $\phi_0$ or equivalently as a constraint on the product
$r_g \cdot \phi$.

## Why this fixes the hierarchical $\phi_g$ failure {#sec-mean-fixes-hier-phi}

With the mean anchor in place, the $(\mu_g, \phi_g)$ degeneracy is broken.
To see this explicitly, consider the mean-odds parameterization with
gene-specific $\phi_g$ from @sec-hierarchical-p.

**Without the anchor**: the model has $2G$ free parameters ($\mu_g$ and
$\phi_g$ for each gene). The likelihood constrains $G$ equations (one mean
per gene) and $G$ variance conditions (one variance-to-mean ratio per gene).
In principle, this system is determined. In practice, the variance conditions
are noisy and the optimizer finds broad ridges in the $(\mu_g, \phi_g)$
space for each gene, creating $G$ independent degeneracy manifolds.

**With the anchor**: the prior on $\mu_g$ effectively fixes $G$ parameters
(up to the slack $\sigma_\mu$). The remaining $G$ free parameters ($\phi_g$)
are constrained by the $G$ variance conditions. The hierarchical prior on
$\phi_g$ provides cross-gene regularization, pulling genes with similar
overdispersion toward a common value. With only one free parameter per gene
($\phi_g$), the optimizer has a well-defined target.

Concretely, the ELBO gradient with respect to $\phi_g$ now receives
unambiguous signal: any change in $\phi_g$ changes the overdispersion
(and hence the likelihood) without being compensated by a change in $\mu_g$
(which is held near $\hat{\mu}_g$ by the prior). The banana-shaped ridges
in $(\mu_g, \phi_g)$ space are replaced by well-defined optima along the
$\phi_g$ axis.

In the canonical parameterization, the situation is analogous: the mean
anchor constrains $r_g \phi$ (or equivalently $r_g (1-p)/p$), so that
changes in $p_g$ directly affect the likelihood through the overdispersion
without being offset by compensating changes in $r_g$.

## Mixture models {#sec-mean-mixture}

### The marginal mean

For a $K$-component mixture model (see @sec-mixture-weights-svi), the
expected observed count for gene $g$ in cell $c$, marginalized over the
component assignment, is

$$
\langle u_{gc} \rangle = \sum_{k=1}^K \gamma_{ck} \cdot \mu_g^{(k)} \cdot
\nu_c,
$${#eq-mean-mixture-expected}

where $\gamma_{ck}$ is the responsibility (posterior probability that cell $c$
belongs to component $k$) and $\mu_g^{(k)}$ is the component-specific
biological mean for gene $g$.

The sample-wide mean for gene $g$ converges to the **marginal mean**:

$$
\bar{u}_g \;\xrightarrow{C \to \infty}\; \bar{\nu} \sum_{k=1}^K
\pi_k \, \mu_g^{(k)},
$${#eq-mean-mixture-convergence}

where $\pi_k$ are the mixing weights. Therefore, the simple anchor

$$
\hat{\mu}_g = \frac{\bar{u}_g}{\bar{\nu}}
$${#eq-mean-mixture-anchor}

estimates the population-averaged mean $\sum_k \pi_k \mu_g^{(k)}$, not any
single component's mean.

### Where the anchor targets in the hierarchical structure

In mixture models, the existing hierarchical mu structure (from
@sec-hdata-hierarchical-mu) decomposes the mean into a population level and
per-component deviations:

$$
\log(\mu_g^{(k)}) = \log(\mu_g) + \tau_\mu \cdot z_{g,k},
\qquad z_{g,k} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1),
$${#eq-mean-mixture-hierarchy}

where $\mu_g$ is the population-level parameter and $\tau_\mu$ controls the
magnitude of per-component deviations. The data-informed anchor targets this
**population-level** $\mu_g$:

$$
\log(\mu_g) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\log(\hat{\mu}_g),
\; \sigma_\mu^2\right).
$${#eq-mean-mixture-prior}

This is the correct level at which to anchor. The population parameter $\mu_g$
represents the "average" expression across components, and the per-component
deviations $\mu_g^{(k)}$ are learned through the hierarchical shrinkage
governed by $\tau_\mu$. The anchor constrains the overall scale while leaving
component-specific variation free.

### Jensen's inequality and the log-normal bias

There is a subtle bias arising from the log-normal structure of the hierarchy.
The marginal arithmetic mean across components is

$$
\sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k \, \mu_g^{(k)} =
\mu_g \sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k \exp(\tau_\mu \, z_{g,k}).
$${#eq-mean-mixture-jensen-lhs}

By Jensen's inequality, since $\exp(\cdot)$ is convex,

$$
\sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k \exp(\tau_\mu \, z_{g,k}) \geq
\exp\!\left(\tau_\mu \sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k \, z_{g,k}\right).
$${#eq-mean-mixture-jensen}

More precisely, the expected value of $\exp(\tau_\mu z_{g,k})$ when
$z_{g,k} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ is $\exp(\tau_\mu^2 / 2)$. This means the
marginal arithmetic mean exceeds the population parameter $\mu_g$ by a factor
of approximately $\exp(\tau_\mu^2 / 2)$:

$$
\left\langle \sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k \, \mu_g^{(k)} \right\rangle \approx
\mu_g \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\tau_\mu^2}{2}\right).
$${#eq-mean-mixture-bias}

For the typical regime where the hierarchical prior does its job ($\tau_\mu$
small), this bias is negligible:

- $\tau_\mu = 0.1$: bias factor $\approx \exp(0.005) = 1.005$ (0.5\%)
- $\tau_\mu = 0.3$: bias factor $\approx \exp(0.045) = 1.046$ (4.6\%)
- $\tau_\mu = 0.5$: bias factor $\approx \exp(0.125) = 1.133$ (13.3\%)

For small to moderate $\tau_\mu$, the bias is well within the tolerance
provided by $\sigma_\mu$. For large $\tau_\mu$ (which indicates genuinely
different expression across components), the bias represents a mild
conservatism: the population parameter $\mu_g$ is slightly below the marginal
mean, which is absorbed by the component-level deviations.

If desired, the bias can be corrected by adjusting the anchor:

$$
\hat{\mu}_g^{\text{corrected}} = \frac{\bar{u}_g}{\bar{\nu}} \cdot
\exp\!\left(-\frac{\tau_\mu^2}{2}\right),
$${#eq-mean-mixture-bias-correction}

but since $\tau_\mu$ is itself a learned parameter, this correction is
typically unnecessary---the model absorbs the small shift into the
hierarchical structure.

### Independence from mixing weight estimation

A notable property of the anchor computation is that it is **completely
independent of the mixing weights**. The sample mean $\bar{u}_g$ is a raw data
average over all cells, with no reference to component assignments,
responsibilities, or mixing weights. This is important in light of the mixing
weight non-identifiability discussed in @sec-mixture-weights-svi: while the SVI
mixing weights can drift to arbitrary values on the flat ELBO landscape, the
anchor $\hat{\mu}_g = \bar{u}_g / \bar{\nu}$ remains well-defined and
correctly determined.

This immunity to the mixing weight pathology is a strength of the approach.
The mean anchor constrains the one quantity that is unambiguously
well-determined by the data (the marginal gene mean), and delegates the
component-level decomposition to the hierarchical structure---which is itself
regularized by the shrinkage prior on $\tau_\mu$.

## Multi-dataset extension {#sec-mean-multi-dataset}

### Population-level anchor

In the multi-dataset hierarchical model from @sec-hierarchical-datasets, the
population-level mean $\mu_g$ is shared across datasets, with dataset-specific
deviations governed by $\tau_\mu$. The mean anchor targets this population
level:

$$
\hat{\mu}_g^{\text{pooled}} = \frac{\bar{u}_g^{\text{pooled}} + \epsilon}{
    \bar{\nu}^{\text{pooled}}
},
$${#eq-mean-multi-anchor}

where $\bar{u}_g^{\text{pooled}}$ is the sample mean of gene $g$ across all
cells from all datasets, and $\bar{\nu}^{\text{pooled}}$ is the average
capture probability across all cells. The prior is then

$$
\log(\mu_g) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(
    \log(\hat{\mu}_g^{\text{pooled}}), \; \sigma_\mu^2
\right).
$${#eq-mean-multi-prior}

Dataset-specific means $\mu_g^{(d)}$ deviate from this population baseline
through the hierarchical structure:

$$
\log(\mu_g^{(d)}) = \log(\mu_g) + \tau_\mu \cdot z_{g,d},
\qquad z_{g,d} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1).
$${#eq-mean-multi-deviation}

The anchor pins the shared baseline; genuine biological differences between
datasets are captured by the $\tau_\mu$ hierarchy.

### Cross-dataset comparability

A key benefit of the population-level anchor in the multi-dataset setting is
that it ensures **cross-dataset comparability** of the inferred parameters.
When two datasets share the same anchor (because they share the same biology),
their $\mu_g$ estimates are placed on a common scale. Differences in library
size or capture efficiency between datasets are correctly attributed to
$\nu_c$ (via the capture prior) and $p^{(d)}$ (via the dataset-specific scalar
$p$), rather than being absorbed into $\mu_g$.

This addresses the parameter degeneracy described in @sec-hdata-motivation:
with $\mu_g$ anchored, the systematic difference in total counts between
datasets must be explained by the technical parameters $\nu_c$ and $p^{(d)}$,
which is where it belongs.

### Multi-dataset mixtures

For multi-dataset mixture models, the anchor targets the population-level
$\mu_g$ that is shared across both datasets and components. The hierarchical
structure provides two layers of deviation: dataset-level (governed by
$\tau_\mu^{\text{dataset}}$) and component-level (governed by
$\tau_\mu^{\text{component}}$). The anchor constrains the top-level parameter,
and the hierarchies partition the variation into its dataset and component
contributions.

## Connection to standard normalization {#sec-mean-normalization}

The data-informed prior on $\mu_g$ has a close relationship to the
normalization methods widely used in single-cell and bulk RNA-seq analysis.

### Size-factor normalization

Methods such as scran, DESeq2, and edgeR compute **size factors** for each
cell (or sample) and divide the observed counts by these factors to obtain
"normalized" counts. In our framework, the size factor for cell $c$ is
essentially $\nu_c$ (the capture probability), and the "normalized" count
for gene $g$ in cell $c$ is

$$
\tilde{u}_{gc} = \frac{u_{gc}}{\nu_c} \approx \mu_g + \text{noise}.
$${#eq-mean-normalized-count}

The average normalized count across cells is then $\bar{\tilde{u}}_g \approx
\mu_g$---precisely our anchor $\hat{\mu}_g$.

The difference between our approach and standard normalization is threefold:

1. **Within the probabilistic model.** Standard normalization is a
   preprocessing step that operates outside the likelihood. Our anchor
   operates within the model as a prior, preserving the full probabilistic
   framework including uncertainty quantification and the nonlinear coupling
   between capture and expression.

2. **Adaptive tightness.** Standard normalization fixes the "normalized mean"
   as a point estimate. Our prior centers on this value but allows deviation
   through $\sigma_\mu$, letting the model refine $\mu_g$ using the full
   count distribution (not just the mean).

3. **Compatible with hierarchical structure.** Standard normalization cannot
   account for hierarchical relationships between datasets, components, or
   conditions. Our anchor integrates naturally with the hierarchical priors
   of @sec-hierarchical-datasets, providing a shared baseline while allowing
   structured deviations.

### Empirical Bayes in genomics

The use of data-derived priors is a hallmark of successful genomics methods.
DESeq2's dispersion shrinkage estimates a prior on the overdispersion from the
marginal distribution of gene-level dispersions. edgeR's empirical Bayes
approach estimates a common dispersion from the data. limma-voom learns the
mean-variance relationship empirically. In each case, the data are used to set
prior hyperparameters that regularize gene-level estimates.

Our mean anchor follows the same philosophy: the sample mean provides a
precise, data-derived center for the prior on $\mu_g$. The width $\sigma_\mu$
controls the degree of shrinkage, just as the prior variance controls
shrinkage in the methods above.

## Deterministic anchoring {#sec-mean-deterministic}

### The deterministic limit

Setting $\sigma_\mu \to 0$ in @eq-mean-prior-def gives a delta-function prior:

$$
\mu_g = \hat{\mu}_g = \frac{\bar{u}_g + \epsilon}{\bar{\nu}}
\quad \text{(deterministic)}.
$${#eq-mean-deterministic}

In this limit, the biological mean expression is no longer a latent
variable---it is a **known function of the data**. The model has zero degrees
of freedom in $\mu_g$: every gene's mean is pinned by its sample mean and
the average capture probability. Only the overdispersion parameters ($\phi$ or
$\phi_g$) remain to be inferred.

### Analogy with deterministic capture anchoring

This parallels the deterministic limit of the capture prior
(@sec-cap-deterministic), where $\sigma_M \to 0$ fixes $\nu_c = L_c / M_0$.
In both cases, the deterministic limit completely eliminates the corresponding
degeneracy by removing the latent variable from the model. The justification
in both cases is a law of large numbers argument: the stochastic variation in
the anchored quantity is negligible compared to the systematic signal.

### When to use the deterministic limit

The deterministic limit is appropriate when:

- The dataset has many cells ($C \gtrsim 1{,}000$) and the sample means are
  precise.
- The goal is to infer overdispersion (e.g., for differential variability
  analysis) rather than to refine mean estimates.
- Computational speed is important: removing $G$ latent variables from the
  model can substantially accelerate inference.

For most applications, the stochastic prior with moderate
$\sigma_\mu \approx 0.3$ is recommended, as it preserves the model's ability
to refine $\mu_g$ while providing strong regularization.

## Mathematical summary {#sec-mean-summary}

We collect the key results of this section.

**Data-informed anchor.** The empirical estimate of the biological mean:

$$
\boxed{
\hat{\mu}_g = \frac{\bar{u}_g + \epsilon}{\bar{\nu}},
\qquad \bar{\nu} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^C \nu_c \approx
\frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^C \frac{L_c}{M_0}.
}
$${#eq-mean-summary-anchor}

**Data-informed prior.** The log-normal prior centered on the anchor:

$$
\boxed{
\log(\mu_g) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\log(\hat{\mu}_g),
\; \sigma_\mu^2\right).
}
$${#eq-mean-summary-prior}

**Concentration bound.** The coefficient of variation of the sample mean:

$$
\boxed{
\text{CV}(\bar{u}_g) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{C}}
\sqrt{\frac{1}{\mu_g \bar{\nu}} + \frac{1}{r_g}}.
}
$${#eq-mean-summary-cv}

Typical values: $1$--$5\%$ for $C = 1{,}000$; sub-percent for $C = 5{,}000$.

**Deterministic limit** ($\sigma_\mu \to 0$):

$$
\boxed{
\mu_g = \hat{\mu}_g = \frac{\bar{u}_g + \epsilon}{\bar{\nu}}.
}
$${#eq-mean-summary-deterministic}

**Mixture model anchor.** For $K$-component mixtures with hierarchical mu:

$$
\boxed{
\log(\mu_g) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\log(\hat{\mu}_g),
\; \sigma_\mu^2\right), \qquad
\log(\mu_g^{(k)}) = \log(\mu_g) + \tau_\mu \cdot z_{g,k}.
}
$${#eq-mean-summary-mixture}

The anchor targets the population-level $\mu_g$; per-component deviations are
governed by $\tau_\mu$. The anchor is independent of mixing weights.

**Multi-dataset anchor.** For $D$-dataset hierarchical model:

$$
\boxed{
\log(\mu_g) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(
    \log(\hat{\mu}_g^{\text{pooled}}), \; \sigma_\mu^2
\right), \qquad
\log(\mu_g^{(d)}) = \log(\mu_g) + \tau_\mu \cdot z_{g,d}.
}
$${#eq-mean-summary-multi}

**Regularization stack.** The complete three-layer regularization:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Layer 1 (capture):} &&
\nu_c \approx L_c / M_0
&& \text{(LLN over genes)} \\
&\text{Layer 2 (mean):} &&
\mu_g \approx \bar{u}_g / \bar{\nu}
&& \text{(LLN over cells)} \\
&\text{Layer 3 (overdispersion):} &&
\phi_g \sim \text{Hierarchical prior}
&& \text{(shrinkage across genes)}
\end{aligned}
}
$${#eq-mean-summary-stack}

Each layer constrains one factor in the multiplicative decomposition of the
expected count $\langle u_{gc} \rangle = \mu_g \cdot \nu_c$, with the
overdispersion parameter $\phi_g$ (or $p_g$) regularized by the hierarchical
prior across genes.
